ontology.foodon_gap_note: No single FoodOn URI covers all mixed green vegetables; FOODON_03301710 (green vegetable food product) used as the broadest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Leafy vegetables) used as the closest defensible category for mixed green vegetables.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No precise SNOMED CT code identified for generic mixed green vegetables without fabricating a code.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of green vegetables is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a side dish portion as commonly served in a Japanese or Singaporean meal context.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetables, mixed, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cites HPB Singapore with energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 2.36 g per 100 g. Protein value of 10.7 g/100 g is unusually high for plain green vegetables (typical range 1–4 g/100 g per USDA). HPB value retained as provided from the canonical HPB source; secondary nutrient values (fibre, sodium, sugar, saturated fat) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for mixed cooked vegetables (FDC ID 170063) as the closest approved-source equivalent. Fat reported as 0.0 g in HPB source; this is confirmed as naturally zero/trace for plain boiled or blanched green vegetables with no added oil. Justification documented per g4 rule.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for generic 'Green Vegetables' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated based on the closest analogous entries for non-starchy green vegetables (e.g., broccoli GI ~10, spinach GI ~15, mixed vegetables GI ~15) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 2.36 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 0.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium, high potassium, magnesium, and dietary nitrates
hypertension.guidance: Green vegetables are naturally low in sodium (~50 mg/100 g) and rich in potassium (~350 mg/100 g) and magnesium, supporting blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH diet principles; patients should avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or oyster sauce during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate, low GI (~15), and moderate dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.36 g carbohydrate per 100 g, a GI of approximately 15, and approximately 2.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g, green vegetables have a negligible glycemic load and are strongly recommended as a staple component of a Type 2 Diabetes meal plan to support postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, high antioxidant vitamins, and anti-inflammatory phytonutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Green vegetables contain negligible saturated fat and cholesterol, and provide vitamins C, E, and K alongside anti-inflammatory phytonutrients such as sulforaphane (in cruciferous varieties), making them cardioprotective and suitable for daily consumption in a heart-healthy diet; preparation without palm oil or lard is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory antioxidants and sulforaphane in cruciferous varieties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Green leafy and cruciferous vegetables provide antioxidants (vitamins C, E, K) and sulforaphane, which reduce inflammatory cytokines; preparation without pro-inflammatory saturated fats is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Vegetables (緑の野菜, Midori no yasai) is a broad category of minimally processed leafy and cruciferous vegetables commonly served as a side dish in Japanese cuisine and across Singaporean food culture. Typical varieties include bok choy, kai lan, broccoli, spinach, and water spinach, prepared by blanching, steaming, or light stir-frying. The nutritional profile per 100 g includes approximately 55 kcal energy, 10.7 g protein (as reported by HPB Singapore — notably higher than typical for plain greens, possibly reflecting a concentrated or mixed preparation), negligible fat (0.0 g, naturally zero for plain preparations without added oil), and 2.36 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is approximately 2.5 g/100 g, sodium is low at ~50 mg/100 g, and potassium is approximately 350 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low GI), making green vegetables an excellent choice for blood glucose management. Clinically, green vegetables are recommended for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, Hypertension, and Cardiovascular Disease due to their low glycemic load, high potassium-to-sodium ratio, and anti-inflammatory phytonutrient content including sulforaphane, vitamins C, E, and K. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Common preparation cautions include avoiding high-sodium sauces and pro-inflammatory cooking fats.
common_user_queries: Are green vegetables good for diabetes?
What is the GI of green vegetables?
How many calories in green vegetables?
Are green vegetables low in sodium?
Can I eat green vegetables if I have hypertension?
What are the health benefits of leafy greens?
Is bok choy good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, carbohydrate, fat) are sourced from HPB Singapore canonical record. The protein value of 10.7 g/100 g is unusually high for plain green vegetables and may reflect a specific HPB preparation or data entry anomaly; it is retained as provided from the canonical source but flagged. Secondary nutrients (fibre, sodium, potassium, vitamins) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for mixed cooked vegetables as the closest approved-source equivalent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Fat_g is 0.0 g: naturally zero for plain blanched or steamed green vegetables with no added oil; confirmed consistent with HPB source and USDA SR Legacy data for plain cooked vegetables. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all 0.0 as naturally zero for this preparation. 2. Protein_g of 10.7 g/100 g is unusually high for plain green vegetables (typical USDA range: 1.5–4.0 g/100 g). This value is retained from the HPB canonical source (confidence 0.95) but is flagged as potentially reflecting a specific HPB food item or data entry that differs from generic green vegetables. Follow-up with HPB source record recommended. 3. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown not available in the HPB source record; USDA SR Legacy values for mixed cooked vegetables do not provide a sufficiently precise match for all these fields given the protein discrepancy. 4. Input food_group was 'Meat and alternatives' and food_subgroup was 'Beef' — these are clearly erroneous for a green vegetable record and have been corrected to 'Vegetables' and 'Leafy and green vegetables' respectively. 5. Input primary_cuisine was 'Japanese'; multilingual naming requirement for Japanese cuisine (Japanese script) has been satisfied with 緑の野菜. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy green vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.
